t113_summary.typed_rule: endpoint-accessible + canonical ambient completion + F(U) subset A(U) + local incomparability
t113_summary.strongest_reading: A typed subobject of H0(G), not raw H0(G), classifies phantom incomparability in the tested finite witness family.
t113_summary.blocked_reading: Raw H0(G), physical torsion, GU validation, and general cohomology/sheafification readings remain blocked.

t92_summary.typed_rule: ambient restriction + audit monotonicity + stable proposition typing
t92_summary.raw_gap_status: not a raw T58 order-pair object; T92 uses typed proposition gaps
t92_summary.strongest_reading: For finite typed proposition-domain accessible-witness systems, G(U)=A(U)-F(U) restricts contravariantly when ambient truth restricts, local auditability is monotone under patch inclusion, and proposition types are not relabeled under restriction. The T19 unary gap and a non-chain joint-witness gap both satisfy these conditions.
t92_summary.blocked_reading: T92 does not identify T19 proposition gaps with T58 order-pair gaps and does not prove a consciousness or complexity-class result.

abstract_schema: finite patch family with restriction maps
ambient object A(U) of content fixed by the larger system
local or auditable subobject F(U) with a declared inclusion into A(U)
gap object G(U)=A(U)-F(U) or its typed subobject
typed admissibility predicate tau selecting meaningful gap sections
restriction closure rho(tau(G(U))) subset tau(G(V)) for V subset U
domain-specific target interpretation kept outside the schema

candidate_evaluations.label: ADMITTED_COMMON_TYPED_GAP_SCHEMA_NO_IDENTITY
candidate_evaluations.reason: Both branches instantiate the same minimal finite schema while keeping carrier kind, target kind, and typing predicates distinct.

candidate.description: T113 and T92 share a finite typed-gap schema: patches, ambient object A, local/auditable subobject F, gap G=A-F, typed admissibility predicate, and restriction closure.
Raw H0(G) is the common classifier for both branches.
T113 and T92 use the same section object.
The bridge promotes T92/T113 into cohomology or physical torsion language.
The bridge promotes T92 into a consciousness or complexity-class claim.
Semantic relabeling may identify proposition gaps with observer-state gaps.
Local reversal/conflict controls may count as typed gaps.

reading: T113 and T92 share a common finite typed-gap schema, but not the same section object, classifier, or theorem target. The useful abstraction is a typed gap-system interface: A, F, G, restriction, and an admissibility predicate tau. The target interpretation stays domain-specific.
no_identity_reasons: T113 carriers are nonreflexive ordered event-pair sections; T92 carriers are unary typed proposition sections.
T113 classifies phantom incomparability witnesses; T92 classifies accessible-witness unauditability gaps.
T113's typing rule is endpoint/canonical/local-incomparability; T92's typing rule is ambient-restriction/audit-monotonicity/stable-proposition-typing.
T113 refutes raw H0(G) as too broad, so a raw-gap identity cannot be the bridge.
T92 explicitly blocks identifying T19 proposition gaps with T58 order-pair gaps.
claim_update: none; C1/gap language may be sharpened only as a finite typed-gap schema shared across distinct section objects
open_blocker: T492 does not prove a general category theorem. A future result would need explicit morphisms between typed gap systems and a natural transformation or equivalence theorem, not just two instances of a schema.
recommended_next: Use the typed-gap schema as a checklist for future T19/T58 bridge attempts.
Do not use raw H0(G), physical torsion, cohomology, consciousness, or complexity-class language from T492.
If this branch continues, define morphisms between typed gap systems and test a third carrier kind.
honest_ceiling: Finite typed-schema bridge only. T492 supports a common typed-gap schema for T113 order-pair phantom gaps and T92 unary proposition gaps, but blocks object identity, raw H0(G) classification, a general cohomology theorem, physical torsion language, consciousness claims, complexity-class claims, claim-ledger movement, public posture, and cross-repo support.
